focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 14

The old sleeper at Retiro Sur held last night's storm in its seams. Water kept sliding out of the roof channels in slow drops that hit the steel step and sounded bigger than they were. Gabriel sat on the stripped lower berth with the sanitation coveralls tied off at his waist, bare forearms on his knees, his fingers still dark with recorder ink that soap never fully took. Someone had once painted clouds on the ceiling panel above him for a tourist route that no longer existed. Rust had eaten through one of the cloud edges and left a brown drip down into a fake blue sky.

"If you don't come back by dark," he said, "I'm not waiting in a graveyard car for sentiment."

Marisol stood in the narrow aisle with her supervisor passcard in one hand and the dead battery radio in the other. The passcard looked wrong to her now, even though it still showed the same laminated face and the same hair pinned back too hard because there had never been time to do it any other way. But the authorization band on the edge had been cut and reissued in amber, escorted access only, Teresa's idea of a courtesy. She had found it clipped to the handle of the service truck at dawn beside a printed note with one sentence on it: `REPORT CALDERA. DO NOT FORCE THIS TO SECURITY.`

"You'll wait," she said.

Gabriel's mouth bent without warmth. "You don't get to say that like I'm twelve."

"No. I get to say it like there are patrol sweeps out there and your ribs are still purple."

He looked away from her toward the grimy window, where the yard beyond the weeds kept flashing pieces of sun off dead rails. In the weak light his left eyebrow scar was a pale cut through the dark hair, old and familiar enough to make her body remember lifting him into a bathtub when he was small and split open fr...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...he went from the reserve car to the property cage because Paloma had told her to and because orders from the people she had failed still felt more solid than anything management said. The cage sat beside the inventory office under failing fluorescent tubes. Plastic pouches hung by case number in long rows that clicked softly when the fans turned on. There were shoes without laces, cheap combs, a hearing aid in a crackled case, and a child's sock stiff with old wash and not enough soap. The corridor could take a person down to four ounces of object in under an hour if it had paperwork ready.

Julián Arce's pouch was still warm from the yard. Someone had tagged it for transfer casualty review and set it apart from the others on a gray tray. Marisol opened it because nobody stopped her and because sometimes rank still worked by muscle memory even when it should not have.

Inside lay seven white tablets folded in wax paper, a pocket comb with two broken teeth, the paper fan made from the meal notice, and three little foil fish flattened nearly smooth from his body weight and the bridge floor. On the outside of the pouch the printer had put the case number in large type and the name beneath in letters half the size.

Marisol took a marker from the desk and wrote `JULIAN ARCE` across the top in block capitals so dark they bled through the plastic. The gesture would not raise him, get Paloma her room back, or wash the smell of hot urine and failed compressions from her hands.

Small, useless, necessary, it kept the day from going neat.

Then she stood under the failing fluorescent light with the basil in one hand and the dead man's property in the other while the train, delayed but not stopped, groaned somewhere beyond the wall like a machine clearing its throat to keep going.
